summary: 9 items surfaced from 22 qualifying stories; 22/22 stories covered and history updated.

items.name: Bid Prowl
items.source_story_title: I aggregated 28 US Government auction sites into one search
items.summary: Searches 28 US government auction sites in one place.
items.why_included: A concrete new product with obvious HN utility.
items.evidence: The thread highlighted normalized listings, deal scoring, and weird inventory examples like helicopters and seized property.

items.name: Honker
items.source_story_title: Durable queues, streams, pub/sub, and a cron scheduler – inside your SQLite file
items.summary: A SQLite-backed queue, streams, pub/sub, and cron system.
items.why_included: It drew direct comparison with established job-queue tools.
items.evidence: Commenters debated the millisecond polling and compared it with Redis, Kine, and Oban.

items.name: Oban
items.source_story_title: Durable queues, streams, pub/sub, and a cron scheduler – inside your SQLite file
items.summary: A background-job framework that now supports SQLite and Postgres.
items.why_included: It surfaced as clear prior art in the Honker discussion.
items.evidence: A commenter called Oban a Postgres-side gold standard and noted SQLite support now exists.

items.name: Granite 4.1
items.summary: IBM's dense models drew praise for local inference and tool use.
items.why_included: It was a fresh model launch with strong practical interest.
items.evidence: Readers called the 8B model impressive for size, fast inference, and structured tasks on local hardware.

items.name: Qwen 3.6
items.summary: Qwen 3.6 was repeatedly praised as a strong coding model.
items.why_included: HN kept returning to it as the better comparison point.
items.evidence: Commenters said Qwen still wins on raw capability for coding and called it a pocket-sized frontier model.

items.name: Firefox
items.source_story_title: LinkedIn scans for 6,278 extensions and encrypts the results into every request
items.summary: The browser commenters recommended to blunt LinkedIn extension fingerprinting.
items.why_included: The thread offered an immediate privacy workaround.
items.evidence: Multiple comments said switching to Firefox or another non-Chromium browser breaks the Chrome-extension scan.

items.name: Wipr
items.source_story_title: LinkedIn scans for 6,278 extensions and encrypts the results into every request
items.summary: A Safari privacy blocker commenters praised as a quiet tracker shield.
items.why_included: It was singled out as a concrete Safari-friendly privacy tool.
items.evidence: A commenter called Wipr a great Safari privacy extension during the LinkedIn discussion.

items.name: NoLimits 2
items.source_story_title: A Primer on Bézier Curves – So What Makes a Bézier Curve?
items.summary: A coaster simulator with curve tools commenters loved.
items.why_included: The discussion called its newer track-building system transformative.
items.evidence: One commenter said No Limits 2's curve system and G-force/banking controls were an absolute game changer.

items.name: Noctua
items.source_story_title: 10Gb/s Ethernet: what I did to get it working in my home
items.summary: Quiet fans commenters recommended to cool hot 10GbE copper modules.
items.why_included: It was a practical hardware fix in a thread full of setup advice.
items.evidence: HN users said 10G copper modules run hot and suggested a Noctua fan or small USB fan.
